text: The surgeon performs a sigmoid resection for diverticulitis using an EEA stapler. Initially, the anastomosis appears intact with no twist or tension. Upon closer inspection, a small tear is visible. A leak test is conducted, showing no bubbles, and a 19mm scope is inserted through the anastomosis, confirming no bleeding. The surgeon examines the anastomosis closely at the end.

The surgeon observes the serosa at the anastomosis site appearing to separate and tear slightly. Using robotic assistance, the surgeon elects to oversew the area, demonstrating the ability to sew 300 degrees circumferentially without tension on the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs over-sewing on the anterior part of the Baker anastomosis between the descending colon and the end of the rectum. The anastomosis is bleeding, possibly due to staples pulling through. The staple line is visible below the needle driver as the surgeon works around the right side of the anastomosis, using patience and traction to achieve a clear posterior view.

The surgeon identifies the rectal staple line on the posterior aspect of the anastomosis and sutures it to the descending colon. The lateral location shows the serosa pulling apart, but the posterior aspect appears intact. The surgeon runs the suture back to lock the barbs in place, completing a 360-degree suture around the anastomosis.

The surgeon runs a suture back around the anterior part of the anastomosis, initially using a backhanded and left-handed technique, then switching to regular suturing as they progress towards the middle and right side of the anastomosis. The suturing is performed to reinforce the anastomosis, ensuring stability and addressing potential issues with the EEA stapler.

And I shudder to think that just diverting a patient in this situation or if there was a bubble leak without making some solid attempt at fixing this, you know, should definitely not be done. And particularly if you have access to a robot, this is definitely a skill that you should be able to do or definitely acquire if you don't yet. Laparoscopically suturing is definitely more of a challenge. I definitely think it's something that we should all practice and get better at. But, you know, overall, I think that the biggest take home for this case is just trying to make sure that we are one hundred percent comfortable before we leave the operating room. And so I'll go ahead and do a leak test again. And I was very happy with the way things ended up. And I hope that there was some learning value in this video for you guys. Thank you.
